sentiment.rationale: The Preacher declares all is vanity; the weariness of labor, the cyclical nature of existence, and the grief of increasing knowledge set a deeply melancholic tone.
Solomon recounts his pursuit of pleasure, wealth, and accomplishment only to declare it all vanity and vexation of spirit.
The famous 'time for everything' passage offers a measured acceptance of God's sovereign ordering, tempered by observations on mortality.
Observes oppression, envy, loneliness, and the futility of labor without companionship, finding companionship as a small consolation.
Warns against rash vows, the love of money, and the vanity of riches, while advising reverence before God.
Laments the man who has wealth but cannot enjoy it, declaring even a stillborn child is better off.
A collection of proverbs favoring sorrow over laughter, patience over pride, and wisdom over folly as moral instruction.
Counsels obedience to the king and trust in God's justice, while acknowledging that the wicked sometimes prosper.
Reflects on death as the great equalizer; urges enjoyment of life as God's gift while acknowledging the uncertainty of time and chance.
Proverbial warnings about folly, laziness, careless speech, and poor leadership contrasted with the benefits of wisdom.
Encourages bold action and generosity in youth while remembering that God will bring all things into judgment.
The allegory of aging and death leads to the final conclusion: fear God and keep his commandments, for God shall judge every work.

summaries.summary: The Preacher introduces the theme of vanity, observing that all human labor is futile under the sun, that nothing is new, and that wisdom itself brings sorrow.
Solomon recounts his experiment with pleasure, building projects, wealth, and wisdom, concluding that all is vanity and that enjoyment of labor is a gift from God.
A famous poem declares a time for every purpose under heaven; God has set eternity in the human heart, yet mortals cannot comprehend the full scope of His work.
Observations on oppression, envy, and the futility of solitary toil lead to the conclusion that companionship and contentment surpass endless striving.
Warns against rash vows and the love of money; the laborer's sleep is sweet, but riches bring anxiety, and enjoyment of one's portion is God's gift.
Laments the tragedy of a man whom God gives wealth and honor but not the ability to enjoy them, declaring that desire is never satisfied.
A collection of wisdom sayings: sorrow is better than laughter, patience better than pride; the chapter warns against extremes and acknowledges the universality of sin.
Counsels respect for authority and trust in God's timing; though sinners may delay punishment, it will surely come, and the righteous should enjoy life as God's gift.
Death comes to all alike; the living should eat, drink, and work with zeal, for there is no knowledge or work in the grave, and time and chance happen to everyone.
Proverbial contrasts between wisdom and folly: a little folly outweighs great wisdom, and careless words and lazy hands bring ruin.
Encourages generosity and bold action in the face of uncertainty; rejoice in youth but remember that God will bring every deed into judgment.
An allegorical description of old age and death concludes with the ultimate charge: fear God and keep his commandments, for this is the whole duty of man.

crossReferences.note: The subjection of creation to vanity echoes the Preacher's declaration that all is vanity.
Both passages affirm that times and seasons are in God's hands.
God's eternal nature is set in the heart of man and evident through creation.
Both warn against using many words in prayer; let words before God be few.
The universal sinfulness of mankind: there is not a just man upon earth that doeth good and sinneth not.
Both passages urge wholehearted effort in whatever one's hand finds to do.
The mystery of the wind and of God's works parallels the mystery of the Spirit's movement.
The spirit returns to God who gave it, reversing the creation of man from dust.
The whole duty of man—to fear God and keep his commandments—echoes Moses' charge to Israel.
God will bring every work into judgment, paralleling the judgment seat of Christ.

verses.reason: 'Vanity of vanities'—the thesis statement of the entire book.
'In much wisdom is much grief'—a paradox on the burden of knowledge.
First of several refrains that enjoyment of labor is from the hand of God.
'To every thing there is a season'—one of the most quoted passages in all of scripture.
God has set eternity in the hearts of men, yet they cannot fathom His work from beginning to end.
'Two are better than one'—a beloved teaching on the value of companionship.
'A threefold cord is not quickly broken'—widely applied to marriage and covenant relationships.
'Let thy words be few'—a call to reverent restraint in the presence of God.
Warns against longing for 'the good old days,' declaring it is not wisdom to do so.
'There is not a just man upon earth, that doeth good, and sinneth not'—universal sinfulness.
'Whatsoever thy hand findeth to do, do it with thy might'—a call to diligent action.
'The race is not to the swift'—a meditation on the unpredictability of life.
'Cast thy bread upon the waters'—a proverb on generosity and faith in uncertain outcomes.
'The spirit shall return unto God who gave it'—a key verse on the nature of the soul.
'Fear God, and keep his commandments: for this is the whole duty of man'—the book's ultimate conclusion.
